title-group.article-title: Machine learning-based Direct Normal Irradiance (DNI) forecasting using satellite data for Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants with Thermal Energy Storage (TES)

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/059qg2m13grid.410588.00000 0001 2191 0132Advanced Institute for Marine Ecosystem Change (WPI-AIMEC), Japan Agency for Marine-Earth Science and Technology (JAMSTEC), 3173-25, Showa-machi, Yokohama-shi, Kanagawa 236-0001 Japan
2https://ror.org/032db5x82grid.170693.a0000 0001 2353 285XClean Energy Research Center, University of South Florida, 4202 E. Fowler Avenue, SVC 1102, Tampa, Florida 33620 United States

To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: A critical requirement of a grid-connected power plant is its ability to adjust power generation in response to changing power demand. Renewable sources, such as solar and wind, are intermittent and require energy storage to dispatch power on demand. Therefore, a feasible energy-storing technology is essential for the sustainable operations of these intermittent sources. Currently, thermal energy storage (TES) is the most viable energy-storing method in the multi-MW range1. Therefore, concentrated solar-thermal power (CSP) plants with integrated thermal energy storage are the most suitable renewable energy options offering dispatchable electricity in the multi-MW range. However, the high levelized cost of energy (LCOE) compared to photovoltaic plants, due to the high investment and operational expenses, makes CSP an uninviting option, even with its dispatchable capabilities. Therefore, a larger number of research studies are being conducted, and many innovations and strategies are being tried out to reduce the LCOE of CSP plants to an appealing level.

Operating a CSP power plant at its maximum available efficiency and dispatching the power on demand with minimal fluctuations is challenging, even with thermal storage, due to the uncertainty in solar irradiation. Solar irradiation should be predicted well in advance to adjust the power cycle’s operating conditions to meet the demand by regulating the flow rates of the network of heat transfer fluids. High fluctuations in power generation could create unexpected supply and demand imbalances, reducing network reliability and security. Although a power network has ancillary operating reserves for correcting these imbalances, high fluctuations make these allocations either insufficient or uneconomical.

A critical advantage of concentrated solar-thermal power plants with integrated thermal energy storage is their enhanced thermal inertia2. Unlike photovoltaic systems, the high heat capacity of TES materials allows these plants to maintain considerable ramp rates and stable power output even during transient DNI fluctuations3,4. Consequently, this thermal buffering capacity reduces the necessity for ultra-precise, high-cost forecasting systems2,5. A forecasting approach with reasonable accuracy, such as the satellite-based framework proposed here, provides sufficient lead time for operators to regulate heat transfer fluid flow rates and optimize dispatch strategies without the economic burden of ground-based cloud-tracking hardware5–7.

Global Horizontal Irradiation prediction is well advanced, and the timely information is well utilized to operate solar photovoltaic plants optimally. Unlike in solar PV, in concentrated solar thermal, only the direct normal irradiance (DNI) is effective, as the diffuse component of the solar radiation cannot be focused or concentrated. Although DNI can be statistically derived from GHI, the results are less reliable as atmospheric conditions such as cloud cover and aerosols affect DNI more strongly than GHI6,8. Various DNI forecasting methods have been introduced in recent years for different horizons with different degrees of accuracy. In general, highly accurate methods are complex and require higher costs for implementation and system operation. DNI Forecasts ranging from horizons 5 minutes to 6 hours ahead are important for CSP plants, depending on the size of the plant, the effectiveness of the heat transfer between fluids, etc.5. For these horizons, ground-based cloud motion vector methods9 and ground-based CMV combined with an artificial neural network10 provide the best accuracy. However, to implement these forecasting methods, a weather station with several high-resolution cloud cameras is required11. Also, the CMV methods require techniques such as block-matching algorithms to determine the cloud coordinates and motion vectors2. The related software and post-processing techniques require knowledgeable and well-trained operators. Therefore, implementing and maintaining such a system may not be cost-effective for all the plants.

It is estimated that CSP plants with TES facilities can have considerable ramp rates, allowing them to control power output within a reasonable time frame, depending on demand3,4,12. Development of thermal storage materials with high thermal conductivity and the innovations that enhance the heat transfer rates between heat-carrying fluids and TES will improve the ramp rates further. The introduction of sCO2 power cycles replacing traditional steam-based cycles in CSP plants will also considerably enhance the ramp rates. The steam turbine is identified as one of the major bottlenecks that prevent higher ramp rates12,13. A higher ramp rate will enable the CSP plant to compensate for any reasonable DNI forecasting error and maintain a committed power generation level, or fine-tune the power output according to demand. In this context, a highly accurate prediction method may not be the optimal or cost-effective choice for all plants. Each forecasting method has its strengths and limitations. The choice of method depends on the nature of the power plant, and a low-cost prediction method with reasonable accuracy may be adequate for many plants.

Geostationary weather satellites provide high spatiotemporal weather data for free, covering a larger part of the world, and therefore, methods that combine machine learning (ML) techniques with satellite data have become a cost-effective and reliable approach for solar radiation forecasting14,15. Additionally, satellite-based methods have demonstrated good performance with forecast horizons of 0–6 hours16, which aligns with the range of horizons of interest in CSP applications. Statistical ML models require real-time ground measurements as complementary inputs to the model. However, with the development of advanced post-processing techniques, near-real-time weather data can also be estimated from satellite data17. Although these estimations may not be as accurate as ground-based measurements, for plants that don’t require an ultra-high level of accuracy, this could be a cost-effective approach that eliminates the need for a ground-based measurement system for ML models. In this context, an improved satellite-based forecasting method with reasonable accuracy could be well suited for CSP plants with TES.

Therefore, in this study, we evaluated and trained 24 ML-based state-of-the-art prediction models using data from the new-generation geostationary Himawari-8 satellite to determine the best accuracy with which we can predict the DNI for the South Asian region for different horizons. It is highly convenient if a model trained and evaluated for one location can be applied to other locations within the same region. In this regard, we systematically changed the latitude and longitude of the original location to represent the entire South Asian region. We evaluated the performance of the trained model for the new locations to examine the model’s adaptability to different locations.

sec.title: Exploratory data analysis
sec.p: The experimental setup for this study encompassed six regions in South Asia: Bhagalpur, Imphal, Jhansi, Kharagpur, Makalu, and Sylhet. Detailed descriptions of these locations are provided in the Methods section.

For each site, the dataset comprised seventeen input variables, excluding the target variable DNI. To identify the most important predictors of solar irradiance, it is essential to examine how the correlation between DNI and each variable changes over time. Figure 1 presents a correlation map that illustrates these temporal dynamics throughout the year.Fig. 1
Monthly Pearson correlation coefficients between measured DNI and twelve atmospheric and radiative variables, displayed as radial bar plots. The top two rows show the six variables with the strongest positive correlations: GHI, clearsky DNI (C-DNI), clearsky GHI (C-GHI), clearsky DHI, C-DHI, DHI, and temperature. In comparison, the bottom two rows show the six variables with the strongest negative correlations: ozone, precipitable water, cloud type, wind direction, relative humidity, and solar zenith angle. The bar color encodes the magnitude of the correlation, ranging from −1 (blue) to +1 (red).

sec.title: Monthly correlation patterns
Forecast skill analysis across lead times
Cross-site performance and topographic impact
sec.p:
In summary, shorter lags, especially 30 minutes, provide the most accurate predictions. GPR remains robust across lags, with fluctuations hinting at periodic trends. These results highlight the trade-off between accuracy and lag duration, underscoring the importance of selecting an optimal lag to balance predictive performance and computational efficiency.Table 2
Computational performance of Exponential GPR and Ensemble Bagged Trees models for DNI prediction.
ModelPrediction Speed (obs/sec)Training Time (sec)Model Size (bytes)
Gaussian Process Regression (Exponential GPR)9 120226.231 081 552
Ensemble (Bagged Trees)19 35116.982 623 603

Table 2 presents the computational performance comparison of the top two models for predicting DNI. The Ensemble Bagged Trees model achieves the highest prediction speed, at 19,351 observations per second, compared to 9,120 observations/second for Exponential GPR, making it preferable for real-time applications. In training time, Bagged Trees require only 16.98 seconds, whereas Exponential GPR needs 226.23 seconds, enabling faster retraining when models must be updated frequently. However, Bagged Trees occupy more storage (2 623 603 bytes) than Exponential GPR (1 081 552 bytes), which may be a constraint in memory-limited environments. Therefore, the choice between these models hinges on whether the need for rapid prediction speed and retraining outweighs the requirement for memory efficiency in the intended application.

sec.title: Evaluation of the case studies
sec.p: In this section, we present the evaluation of each case study using scatter plots at lag times ranging from 30 minutes to 360 minutes. Furthermore, for each site, we compute the average SHAP score to identify the most impactful features in our model’s predictions.

caption.p: Scatter plots illustrate the predicted versus actual DNI values for different lag times – Bhagalpur Case. Subplots (a) to (f) represent lag times of 30 minutes to 180 minutes in 30-minute intervals, respectively. The diagonal line in each plot represents the ideal prediction, where predicted values perfectly align with actual values.

Scatter plots illustrate the predicted versus actual DNI values for different lag times – Bhagalpur Case. Subplots (a) to (f) represent lag times of 210 minutes to 360 minutes in 30-minute intervals, respectively. The diagonal line in each plot represents the ideal prediction, where predicted values perfectly align with actual values.
Temporal variation of the six most influential SHAP features across 30 to 360 minute lags for Ahamadab.

sec.p: Figure 4 comprises six radial bar charts for the top SHAP features: Clearsky DHI, Cloud Type, Dew Point, Solar Zenith Angle, Precipitable Water, and lagged DNI. Bars are plotted at angular positions corresponding to lag times of 30, 60,..., 360 minutes. Each bar’s length represents the mean absolute SHAP value of that feature at the given lag, and black boxed white labels display the exact values.
All case studies were conducted using the same modeling framework and analysis procedure as applied for the Bhagalpur case.

sec.title: Performance evaluation across lag times using RMSE and Bias metrics

caption.p: RMSE and Mean Bias comparison across twelve lag times (30 to 360 minutes) for five stations: (a) Impal, (b) Jhansi, (c) Sylhet, (d) Kharagpur, and (e) Makalu. The height indicates the RMSE, while bar color represents the Mean Bias (Predicted – Actual), with red denoting overestimation and blue denoting underestimation. The visualization highlights variations in model accuracy and directional error across spatial and temporal dimensions.

sec.p: Figure 5 illustrates a comprehensive comparison of RMSE and mean bias across twelve lag times for the five stations: Impal, Jhansi, Sylhet, Kharagpur, and Makalu. In each subplot, the bar height represents RMSE, and the color shading encodes the mean bias, with blue tones indicating underestimation and red tones indicating overestimation of DNI values. For Impal, the model demonstrates relatively high RMSE at 60 minutes to 120 minutes and lower RMSE at 180 minutes and 270 minutes; however, these lower RMSE values coincide with strong negative bias, indicating underprediction, while a positive bias at 60 minutes signifies overprediction, revealing a trade-off between error magnitude and directional bias. In Jhansi, RMSE remains consistent across all lag times, and mean bias stays near zero, suggesting balanced predictions with slight overpredictions at 150 minutes and 360 minutes, making Jhansi one of the most stable stations. Sylhet exhibits a modest RMSE, with a slight peak occurring between 120 minutes and 180 minutes. The bias is mainly negative but small in magnitude, indicating slightly conservative predictions. In Kharagpur the lowest RMSE occurs at 60 minutes with gradual increases at longer lags, and bias remains neutral across the range, demonstrating reliable and directionally correct performance at short to mid-range lag times. For Makalu, the RMSE peaks at 180 minutes and is lowest at 30 minutes, with a significant positive bias at 180 minutes, indicating strong overprediction. The bias alternates between overestimation and underestimation at other lags, suggesting erratic model behavior. Overall, these results underscore station- and lag-dependent variations in performance, highlighting the importance of tailoring lag selection to station-specific characteristics to optimize forecast accuracy and minimize systematic errors.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study aimed to predict DNI across six diverse regions in South Asia using ML models, specifically assessing how lagged atmospheric variables impact forecasting accuracy. Exploratory analysis revealed strong positive correlations between DNI and GHI, as well as clear-sky parameters (C-DNI, C-GHI, and C-DHI), DHI, and temperature, particularly during the late winter to early spring months when atmospheric turbidity and moisture levels are at their lowest. Conversely, negative correlations were strongest for ozone, PW, CT, WD, RH, and SZA, emphasizing the significant role of atmospheric moisture, clouds, and solar geometry in irradiance attenuation.

Performance evaluations using lagged variables indicated that short lag intervals, particularly 30 minutes, provided the highest prediction accuracy. Ensemble Bagging Regression demonstrated superior performance at 30-minute lags, while GPR maintained robust and consistent predictive accuracy for longer lags. Notably, prediction accuracy exhibited periodic fluctuations at certain extended intervals, suggesting exploitable atmospheric cycles, which highlights the importance of carefully selecting optimal lag times based on predictive goals and computational constraints.

Case study analyses further revealed nuanced differences in model performance tied to local geographical and climatological factors. Stations in flatter terrain, such as Jhansi and Kharagpur, consistently exhibited stable and high predictive accuracy, suggesting that models developed for plains with similar climates can reliably generalize to other flat regions. Conversely, mountainous and topographically complex sites (Makalu, Imphal, Sylhet) showed higher variability and distinct nonlinear predictive behavior. Makalu, located near the high peaks of the Himalayas, exhibited the most significant fluctuations and biases in predictions, underscoring that complex terrain has a substantial impact on the model’s predictive uncertainty. These findings strongly suggest that topography plays a crucial role in the model’s ability to generalize across regions.

sec.title: Outlook and future work
sec.p: Given the observed variability in predictive accuracy related to topographic complexity, future model development should consider incorporating additional terrain-specific parameters and local atmospheric phenomena to enhance predictive accuracy. It is recommended that separate modeling frameworks be developed for mountainous and plain regions, possibly using hybrid approaches that combine satellite-derived data, numerical weather models, and local observational datasets to improve accuracy in regions with complex topography. Furthermore, additional research is needed to evaluate the impact of aerosol distributions and localized climatic variations, which could significantly enhance model robustness and predictive reliability.

The findings of this study are particularly relevant for the operational economics of CSP-TES plants. While ground-based methods may offer higher precision for sub-10-minute intervals, the 30-to-360-minute horizons addressed by our Exp-GPR model align with the dispatch planning cycles of storage-coupled plants5,16. The ability of TES to compensate for moderate forecasting errors5 justifies the shift toward satellite-derived data, which offers a scalable and economically prudent solution for reducing the LCOE in the South Asian renewable sector12,13,15.

sec.title: Site selection
Data collection
sec.p: The primary data source for this study is the geostationary Himawari-8 satellite, operated by the Japan Meteorological Agency (JMA). Its Advanced Himawari Imager (AHI) captures full-disk Earth images every 10 minutes with spatial resolutions of 0.5 km to 2 km22. These high-resolution observations are integrated into the National Solar Radiation Database (NSRDB), which provides a standardized 30-minute temporal resolution dataset23.

To clarify the relationship between satellite observations and the physical parameters used in our models, Table 4 details the 17 input variables extracted. While the radiative components (DNI, GHI, DHI) are derived from the AHI sensor’s spectral channels, meteorological parameters such as Precipitable Water and Wind Speed are integrated into the NSRDB via reanalysis data to provide a comprehensive atmospheric profile.Table 4
Summary of meteorological and radiative variables used for model training.
Variable CategorySpecific ParametersPrimary SourceResolution
Radiative FluxesDNI, GHI, DHI, Clearsky (DNI, GHI, DHI)Himawari-8/NSRDB2 km/30-min
Atmospheric StateCloud Type (CT), Dew Point, Rel. Humidity (RH)Himawari-8/NSRDB2 km/30-min
MeteorologicalWind Speed (WS), Wind Direction (WD), TempNSRDB Reanalysis2 km/30-min
Geophysical/OtherSolar Zenith Angle (SZA), Precip. Water (PW), OzoneNSRDB2 km/30-min

By utilizing satellite-derived data, the proposed forecasting framework can be deployed at any geographic location within the Himawari-8 footprint without requiring expensive ground-based measurement stations, significantly reducing the LCOE for CSP plants

sec.title: Data preprocessing
Machine learning models
sec.p: In this study, twenty-four state-of-the-art machine learning models were evaluated for DNI prediction. To ensure the robustness of the results and prevent overfitting, a chronological validation strategy was employed. Unlike standard k-fold cross-validation, which may ignore temporal dependencies, the dataset was split into Training, Validation, and Test sets while strictly preserving their original time order24. The Training set was used for initial model fitting, the Validation set for systematic hyperparameter tuning, and the Test set for the final, unbiased evaluation of the forecasting framework’s skill across various lead times19.

sec.title: Exponential Gaussian Process Regression (GPR)
Bagged trees
sec.p:
The hyperparameter settings for both GPR and Bagged trees are given in Table 5.Table 5
Hyperparameters for the selected models.
ModelHyperparameters
Gaussian Process Regression (Exponential GPR)Basis function: constant; kernel function: exponential; use isotropic kernel: yes; kernel scale: automatic; signal standard deviation: automatic; sigma: automatic; standardize data: yes; optimize numeric parameters: yes
Ensemble (Bagged Trees)Minimum leaf size: 8; number of learners: 30; number of predictors to sample: select all

sec.title: Evaluation metrics
Explainability techniques
sec.p: Understanding the inner workings of machine learning models is crucial for ensuring trust and interpretability, particularly in critical applications such as DNI prediction. This study employs Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to analyze feature importance, providing a transparent framework for quantifying the contributions of individual features. SHAP is based on cooperative game theory and assigns each feature a Shapley value, representing its fair contribution to the model’s prediction by considering all possible feature combinations25,26.

Complex models such as Exponential GPR and deep neural networks are often viewed as “black boxes.” SHAP bridges this gap by providing both global and local explanations: summary plots highlight overall feature importance, while dependence plots show how individual features affect the model output27. By quantifying the impact of variables such as Solar Zenith Angle and Clearsky DNI, SHAP provides actionable insights for stakeholders in renewable energy systems.

Employing SHAP enhances model interpretability and aligns with the growing demand for explainable AI in high-stakes domains28. The combination of rigorous theoretical grounding and intuitive visualizations makes SHAP an ideal tool for evaluating and communicating feature importance in DNI forecasting models.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Namal Rathnayake and Samantha Wijewardane are contributed equally to this work.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s41598-026-41733-7.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: N.R conceived the experiment(s), N.R. and S.W. conducted the experiment(s). N.R. and S.W. analysed the results. All authors reviewed the manuscript.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data used in this study are publicly available from the National Solar Radiation Database (NSRDB), maintained by the U.S. National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL). The datasets can be accessed at: https://nsrdb.nrel.gov/data-sets/how-to-access-data. Users must register for a free account to download the data. All data used in the analysis were obtained in accordance with NSRDB’s data use guidelines.

notes.title: Code availability
notes.p: The full implementation code used for data preprocessing, model training (Exp-GPR and Bagged Trees), and the generation of results presented in this study is available in the Zenodo repository. The code is open-source and can be accessed via the permanent Digital Object Identifier (DOI): https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18681575.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
